questions.question: Why does the Cat’s-food-Man believe Dr. Dolittle is uniquely suited to be an animal doctor?

options.A: Because the Doctor has a lot of money to buy medicine
options.B: Because he understands the 'nature' of animals better than common vets
options.C: Because the Doctor is too old to treat humans anymore
options.D: Because his house is already full of stray pets

questions.explanation: The Cat's-food-Man notices that the Doctor treats animals with the same respect and intelligence as human patients, which was rare for that time.

questions.question: What was the 'turning point' that led Polynesia to reveal the secret of animal language to the Doctor?

options.A: The Doctor saved her life from a cat
options.B: The Doctor complained that 'horses are stupid'
options.C: The Doctor admitted he was becoming poor and needed a new job
options.D: The Doctor asked why the birds were singing so loudly

questions.explanation: When the Doctor mistakenly calls animals 'stupid' for not speaking, Polynesia corrects him and explains they have their own complex languages.

questions.question: How does Polynesia define the difference between human language and animal language?

options.A: Animal language is just simpler and shorter
options.B: Animal language is based on feelings rather than facts
options.C: Human language uses only words, while animal language uses the whole body
options.D: There is no difference; animals just choose not to speak to humans

questions.explanation: Polynesia explains that animals communicate through vocalizations, tail movements, ear twitches, and scents.

questions.question: Which animal was responsible for the Doctor's financial ruin as a 'human doctor'?

options.B: The hedgehog
options.C: The parrot
options.D: The mice

questions.explanation: A rheumatic old lady sat on a hedgehog that was sleeping on the sofa, which caused the townspeople to stop coming to the clinic.

questions.question: What does the plough horse tell Dr. Dolittle about other 'vets' (horse-doctors)?

options.B: They treated horses with medicine that didn't work
options.C: They were 'thick-headed' because they didn't understand what was actually wrong
options.D: They were afraid of big horses

questions.explanation: The horse explains that other vets didn't listen or realize his eyes were failing; they just treated him for the wrong problems.

questions.question: What is the significance of the 'Green Spectacles' in the story?

options.A: They were a fashion statement for the horse
options.B: They represent the Doctor's ability to solve problems by listening to his patients
options.C: They were magic glasses that allowed the horse to see the future
options.D: They protected the horse's eyes from the sun

questions.explanation: By listening to the horse's specific complaint about his vision, the Doctor provides a solution that no other vet had considered.

questions.question: Why does Sarah Dolittle threaten to leave the house and get married?

options.B: She was tired of cleaning up after the animals and losing money
options.C: She wanted to open her own clinic
options.D: She was afraid of the parrot

questions.explanation: Sarah represents the practical, human-centric view that the Doctor's love for animals is becoming a burden.

questions.question: What can we infer about Polynesia’s age and wisdom?

options.A: She is a young bird who just started talking
options.B: She is very old and has traveled the world, giving her a deep understanding of many cultures
options.C: She is 10 years old
options.D: She learned everything from books in the Doctor's library

questions.explanation: Polynesia mentions having been to Africa and seeing many things, which explains why she is the one to mentor the Doctor.

questions.question: How does the Doctor’s house change once he becomes a vet?

options.A: It becomes very clean and organized
options.B: It is redesigned with special entrances and comforts for different animal species
options.C: He sells all his furniture to buy animal food
options.D: It becomes a zoo where people pay to enter

questions.explanation: He creates a 'home' rather than just a clinic, making sure mice, birds, and larger animals all feel welcome.

questions.question: When Polynesia tells the Doctor to 'keep a ledger' of animal sounds, she is encouraging him to be more:

options.B: Imaginative and poetic
options.C: Fast and efficient
options.D: Wealthy and famous

questions.explanation: Learning a new language requires study and documentation, treating animal communication as a real subject of study.

questions.question: Which animal acts as the Doctor’s 'housekeeper' after Sarah leaves?

options.B: Polynesia the parrot
options.C: Jip the dog
options.D: Gub-Gub the pig

questions.explanation: Dab-Dab takes over the keys and the household management, showing that animals can be just as capable as humans.

questions.question: What does the phrase 'Puddleby-on-the-Marsh' tell us about the setting?

options.B: It is a wet, low-lying coastal or river-side town
options.C: It is a mountain village
options.D: It is a city in the sky

questions.explanation: A 'marsh' is a wetland, and the name suggests a small, perhaps muddy, traditional English town.

questions.question: What is the primary reason the Doctor becomes poor?

options.B: He prioritized his passion for helping animals over making a profit from humans
options.C: Thieves stole his money
options.D: He spent all his money on books

questions.explanation: The Doctor is a character of high integrity who follows his heart rather than financial gain.

questions.question: How did the monkeys in Africa know that the animals in England were being treated well?

options.B: The swallows told them during their migration
options.C: The monkeys read it on the internet
options.D: The Doctor visited Africa first

questions.explanation: Birds act as the global 'news network' for the animal kingdom in this story.

questions.question: What does the Doctor do when he runs out of money for animal food?

options.B: He stops eating himself and lets the animals have his food
options.C: He asks his sister for money
options.D: He starts a garden to grow his own food

questions.explanation: His selflessness is shown by his willingness to sacrifice his own comfort for the creatures under his care.

questions.question: Why was the organ-grinder’s monkey (Chee-Chee) unhappy before meeting the Doctor?

options.B: He was treated like a toy and not a thinking being
options.C: He wanted to be a human
options.D: He was cold all the time

questions.explanation: The story critiques the way humans use animals for entertainment without considering their well-being.

questions.question: Polynesia mentions that birds have a 'very complicated' language. What makes it so?

options.A: They use thousands of different whistles and chirps
options.B: The meaning changes depending on the way they fluff their feathers
options.C: They only speak in code
options.D: Only female birds can talk

questions.explanation: She explains that visual cues are just as important as the sounds themselves in bird language.

questions.question: What kind of reputation does Dr. Dolittle build among the animals?

options.A: A doctor who is very scary
options.B: A man who is 'one of them' and understands their needs
options.C: A human who wants to cage them
options.D: A man who gives out free candy

questions.explanation: The animals trust him because he treats them as equals and listens to their language.

questions.question: The Doctor’s decision to learn animal language is an example of _____.

options.B: Empathy and lifelong learning
options.C: Laziness
options.D: Greed

questions.explanation: He is willing to learn something entirely new to better help those he cares about.

questions.question: Which animal is known as the 'greatest gourmet' in the Doctor’s house?

options.B: Dab-Dab the duck
options.C: Too-Too the owl
options.D: Chee-Chee the monkey

questions.explanation: Gub-Gub's character is defined by his love for vegetables and fine food.

questions.question: Why did the Doctor keep a 'crocodile' in his garden pond?

options.B: Because it was sick and had nowhere else to go
options.C: To scare away his sister
options.D: To show off to the neighbors

questions.explanation: He takes in animals that others find dangerous or unappealing, showing his unbiased kindness.

questions.question: What does the word 'rheumatism' refer to in the story?

options.B: A disease causing pain in joints and muscles
options.C: A small town in England
options.D: The Doctor's favorite book

questions.explanation: The old lady who sat on the hedgehog had rheumatism, a common ailment for elderly people.

questions.question: The 'Cat’s-food-Man' serves as a 'foil' to the other townspeople because:

options.A: He is richer than the others
options.B: He sees the Doctor's value while others see him as a failure
options.C: He hates animals more than the others
options.D: He is the Doctor's only friend

questions.explanation: While the rest of Puddleby thinks the Doctor is strange, the Cat's-food-Man recognizes his genius.

questions.question: How does the author use 'humor' in the story?

options.A: By making the animals act like grumpy old people
options.B: By describing the chaotic and funny situations of a house full of animals
options.C: By telling jokes through the narrator
options.D: By making the Doctor look foolish

questions.explanation: The humor comes from the logic of animals interacting with human domestic life (like a duck with a ring of keys).

questions.question: Which of these is a 'climax' of the Doctor's transition to a vet?

options.B: The moment he successfully fits the horse with spectacles
options.C: The first time he sees a parrot
options.D: When he decides to move to Africa

questions.explanation: This event proves that his new method of 'listening' to animals works better than traditional medicine.
